humanities_role_explanation: Evaluates general-purpose multimodal models on a domain-specific task (interpreting and classifying Christian iconography) using visual and textual art-historical data.
benchmark_modalities_explanation: Combines images of religious paintings with text prompts containing class labels or detailed Iconclass descriptions.
analytical_tasks_explanation: Factual and visual recognition of Christian saints in art, tested via zero-shot classification with label names, zero-shot classification with rich iconographical descriptions, and in-context learning with five exemplars.

general_purpose_benchmark.note: Specifically designed to evaluate domain-specific performance on Christian iconography, not a general-purpose vision-language benchmark.

evaluates_generative_llm.note: Evaluates OpenAI GPT-4o, GPT-4o-mini, Google Gemini 2.5 Pro, and Gemini 2.5 Flash.

llm_is_primary_system.note: Multimodal generative LLMs and contrastive VLMs are the primary systems being benchmarked.

arts_humanities_data.note: Focuses on Visual Arts and Performing Arts (ASJC 1213) and Religious Studies, evaluating Christian religious paintings annotated with Iconclass codes.

quantitative_metrics.note: Uses classification Accuracy and cross-dataset consistency (prediction stability on near-duplicate images).

published_after_march_2023.note: The paper is a preprint from September 2025.

english_language.note: The paper and prompt templates are written in English.

summary: This study benchmarks the performance of Vision-Language Models and Multimodal Large Language Models on single-label image classification of Christian iconography. Using refined subsets of ArtDL, ICONCLASS, and Wikidata, the authors test models across zero-shot and few-shot settings. The research highlights the potential of general-purpose LLMs as metadata curation tools in digital humanities while showing limitations under noisy data and few-shot conditions.
key_findings: Gemini 2.5 Pro outperformed all models on the ArtDL and ICONCLASS datasets, achieving peak accuracies of 90.45% and 84.82% respectively. On the Wikidata dataset, performance dropped significantly for all models, with SigLIP-SO400M-Patch14-384 achieving the highest accuracy of 66.29%, outperforming the LLMs which hovered around 45%. Zero-shot classification with rich descriptions generally improved accuracy for contrastive models but had mixed results for LLMs, while few-shot learning rarely improved performance and often degraded it.
